title: Day 3 flow engineering pattern
content: Day 3 flow engineering uses Microsoft Agent Framework Workflows to make a process explicit. The flow includes intake, classification, branching, human approval, retry, workflow state, and typed final output. This is useful when enterprise work must be explainable and controllable rather than hidden inside one prompt.
relevanceHint: Use for questions about flow engineering, workflow steps, branching, HITL, retry, and typed outputs.

title: Day 3 agent skills lifecycle
content: Agent skills in Day 3 are taught as discovery, activation, execution, and reuse. The starter pack includes local file-based skills, inline skills, class-based skills, and API Center registered skill packages. API Center is the catalog and governance layer. Runtime loading through UseMcpSkills requires an MCP server that exposes skill packages.
relevanceHint: Use for questions about file skills, inline skills, class skills, API Center skills, MCP skill publication, and reusable skill boundaries.

title: Day 3 state and memory model
content: State and memory are separated into session continuity, context providers, storage, serialization, and compaction. The Day 3 lab uses AgentSession, StateBag, context providers, chat history storage, session serialization, and compaction. Cosmos DB is the enterprise persistence upgrade path for serialized sessions and operational records.
relevanceHint: Use for questions about AgentSession, StateBag, context providers, storage, serialization, compaction, and Cosmos persistence.

title: Day 3 harness engineering
content: Harness engineering uses the official Microsoft Agent Framework Harness. The harness manages the runtime around the model: tool exposure, tool approval, loop evaluators, context compaction, file memory, file access, todo and mode providers, OpenTelemetry configuration, and evidence capture. The lab must use AsHarnessAgent with HarnessAgentOptions.
relevanceHint: Use for questions about harness runtime, tool approval, loop evaluators, file memory, OpenTelemetry, and evidence capture.

title: Retrieval choices for AI-Native RAG
content: RAG is not one Azure service. Azure AI Search is a dedicated enterprise search and indexing service, strong for document search and hybrid search. Cosmos DB is a cost-effective operational data grounding option for JSON and structured records, and can also participate in vector search designs. HorizonDB is a PostgreSQL-native AI database direction that supports pgvector, full-text search, hybrid search, embeddings, DiskANN, reranking, and GraphRAG concepts, but it is currently concept-only for this batch because of cost and regional availability.
relevanceHint: Use for questions comparing Azure AI Search, Cosmos DB, HorizonDB, vector retrieval, hybrid retrieval, and cost-effective RAG.

title: Cosmos DB grounding pattern
content: For Day 3 Lab 06, Cosmos DB is the hands-on retrieval backend. The agentic workflow queries structured training knowledge records by batch and student, ranks evidence by query relevance, returns cited records to the model through retrieve_training_context, verifies citations, and retries once when grounding is weak. This keeps the workflow enterprise-relevant without requiring an always-on Azure AI Search service.
relevanceHint: Use for questions about Cosmos DB grounding, structured records, cost control, citations, and the retrieval tool boundary.

title: Day 3 workflow agent bridge
content: The Day 3 workflow agent composes Foundry-backed specialist agents into a structured business process. The Intent Analyst, Planning Agent, Risk Reviewer, and Finalizer Agent are orchestrated with AgentWorkflowBuilder.CreateSequentialBuilderWith. This bridges Day 3 single-agent workflow patterns into Day 4 multi-agent orchestration.
relevanceHint: Use for questions about workflow agents, specialist roles, structured output, and the Day 3 to Day 4 transition.

title: Day 4 multi-agent coordination
content: Day 4 compares sequential, concurrent, handoff, and group-chat coordination patterns using native Microsoft Agent Framework workflow builders. Sequential coordination is useful when order matters. Concurrent coordination is useful when independent specialists can work in parallel. Handoff is useful when a router chooses the next specialist. Group chat is useful for bounded shared discussion.
relevanceHint: Use for questions about multi-agent architecture, coordination patterns, handoff, concurrent work, and group chat.

title: A2A and API Center distinction
content: A2A is the runtime interoperability boundary for agent-to-agent communication. The provider exposes an Agent Card and message endpoint. The consumer resolves the Agent Card and sends a message using the A2A SDK. Azure API Center catalogs the agent asset with owner, lifecycle, source, compatibility, and runtime endpoint metadata, but API Center is not the runtime broker.
relevanceHint: Use for questions about A2A, Agent Card, API Center, runtime endpoint, discovery, and consumption.

title: AgentGateway control point
content: AgentGateway is the runtime control point between AI-native applications and model, tool, and agent backends. Day 4 uses it to discuss routes for model calls, MCP tool calls, and A2A agent calls. Gateway policy can add logging, rate limiting, identity, headers, trace correlation, and cost attribution.
relevanceHint: Use for questions about gateway routing, model route, MCP route, A2A route, rate limiting, cost tags, and observability.

title: Alpha batch retrieval cost decision
content: For the alpha batch, Azure AI Search was removed from the Day 3 runtime requirement because the service is billed per hour and is costly to keep alive across weekends. Cosmos DB serverless is used for hands-on structured grounding. HorizonDB is covered as a concept because it is region-limited and expensive for every student to run during the alpha batch.
relevanceHint: Use for questions about why Cosmos DB is used hands-on, why Azure AI Search is not required, and why HorizonDB is concept-only.
